6. Memory and Sequence Modeling (Kaiming He)

6.1. CNNs do not suffice

CNNs can process sequences with time as a dimension. Switch the convolution to be casual so that you don’t look into the future.

  • Pros: They are parallelizable, time translation equivariant, and good at detecting short-term patterns.
  • Cons: However, they have a finite receptive fields and no memory.

6.2. RNNs

RNNs are used to model state machines, where the state involves in time according to the current input and previous state (Markov property), and the output depends on the current state. We can implement the evolution and output processes as neural networks. We learned an alternative modular architecture in class.

The state encodes memory, some representation of the inputs up to the current time.

  • A deep RNN can be made by stacking hidden layers. This is better than using an MLP on the concatenated vector since that mixes the temporal recurrence and depth in a single transformation. Is more intuitive to apply a modular function. The weights are shared horizontally to motivate time-translation equivariance.

    • [TODO] Concatenation + matrix allows for better mixing
  • We can perform backprop given labels for .
  • RNNs make a Markov assumption on hidden states. If designed correctly then they can propapagate signals in memory.
  • RNN also run into problems with long-range dependencies: The chance of a signal surviving many matrix multiplications is low. Simularly, gradients mayvanish or explode.

6.3. LSTM

  • LSTM (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber, 1997) is parsed as

    • Memory = representation of past
    • Short-term memory = Markov: only access nearby states
    • Long {Short-term} memory = the short-term memory can retain information across a long time

  • Mechanism

    • Very good blog post: http://colah.github.io/posts/2015-08-Understanding-LSTMs/

    • Cell state maintains long term memory (disk) and the hidden state maintains short term memory (RAM).

      • The LSTM allows the cell state to pass linearly through, hence it can preserve long range dependencies.
    • Hidden state and input update cell state. Cell state updates input state using an analogous mechanism

6.4. Application: Autoregressive Models

Autoregressive models: predicting next word in sentence. Take a casual model (CNN/RNN/LSTM/Transformer) and run a classifier head on the hidden state to get an output token. 435

  • During training we want to maximize likelihood of final sentence (product of conditional likelihoods). Teacher forcing: feed ground truth preceding word instead of the generated word during training so that the model learns all conditional dependencies. The downside is that at test time (inference), the model does feed on its own predictions. The idea is that with enough training and data it doesn’t matter.

    • During a forward pass you sum the cross entropy loss over all time steps to obtain log-loss of the whole sequence.

Autoregressive models can be done with CNNs, RNNs, and attention.

For example: PixelRNN and PixelCNN
